summary:
The Partition of Bengal in 1905, ordered by the British Viceroy Lord Curzon, divided the large and densely populated Bengal presidency into two provinces — East Bengal and Assam (with a Muslim majority) and West Bengal (with a Hindu majority). The partition triggered a massive political backlash that became a turning point in Indian nationalism and the development of both the Indian National Congress and, eventually, the Muslim League.

Curzon justified the partition on administrative grounds — Bengal was indeed very large — but many Bengalis and Indian nationalists interpreted it as a deliberate British attempt to divide the Bengali people along religious lines and weaken the politically active Bengali intelligentsia that had been a leading force in the nationalist movement. The partition coincided with the anniversary of the [[event:2563|British Raj's establishment]] in ways that heightened its symbolic charge.

The response was the Swadeshi movement — a boycott of British goods and promotion of Indian-made products that was the most significant mass protest movement in colonial India up to that point. Huge public demonstrations, bonfires of British cloth, and the wearing of rough homespun fabric (khadi) as a political statement prefigured the methods Gandhi would later make famous. The movement gave Indian nationalism a mass character it had previously lacked and stimulated Indian industrial development.

The partition was revoked in 1911, a significant political concession by the British that demonstrated the power of organized nationalist pressure. However, the episode had deepened both Hindu-Muslim communal tensions (some Muslims had supported the partition that created a Muslim-majority province) and nationalist consciousness, contributing to the political developments that would ultimately lead to independence — and to the actual partition of Bengal and India in 1947.
